sections.text: Welcome to AP Computer Science A. Module One — primitive types. This is where every Java program begins. By the end of this lesson you'll know how Java stores numbers, decimals, true-false values, and characters, and how to print all of them.

Here it is. Your first Java program. Five words, one semicolon, and the computer talks back to you. System dot out dot println, open paren, the text Hello GIIS in quotes, close paren, semicolon. That line is the whole hello-world. Today we unpack everything inside it — and the four types of data that fill the parentheses in real programs.

Three things to cover. One — what makes Java tick. Compiled language, bytecode, JVM. Two — the primitive types you'll use every single day: int, double, boolean, and char. Three — how to declare variables, how casting works, and how to print results. Standard CS A territory.

Java is a compiled language. You write your code in a dot-java file. The compiler turns it into dot-class bytecode. The JVM — the Java Virtual Machine — runs that bytecode. Why bother? Speed plus safety. Java was built with the slogan write once, run anywhere — same bytecode on Windows, Mac, or Linux.

Primitives are the simplest things Java stores. They hold a value directly — not a reference, not an object, just the value itself. There are eight in the language, but the four you'll use most are: int for whole numbers, double for decimals, boolean for true or false, and char for a single character.

First two — int and double. Int holds whole numbers. age equals seventeen. Done. Double holds decimals. pi equals three point one four. Quick range note. An int can go up to about two billion. If you need bigger whole numbers — say population counts or large IDs — you use long instead. For this module, int is fine.

Next two — boolean and char. A boolean is true or false, nothing else. isOpen equals true. A char holds a single character — one letter, one symbol — written with single quotes. grade equals capital A in single quotes. Watch this. Single quotes for char. Double quotes for String. Mix them up and the compiler will tell you immediately.

Every variable declaration follows the same pattern. Type, then name, then equals, then value, then semicolon. The semicolon is not optional — it ends every statement in Java. score equals ninety-five. rate equals zero point zero seven five. Same shape every time.

Your turn. Declare three variables. pi — a decimal, three point one four. isOpen — a boolean, true. count — a whole number, five. Pick the right primitive type for each one. Pause now, write the three lines, press play when you're ready.

Time. Pi is a decimal, so double pi equals three point one four. isOpen is true or false, so boolean isOpen equals true. count is a whole number, so int count equals five. Three types, three lines, three semicolons. That's the rhythm of Java.

Casting. Two flavors. First — explicit cast. int x equals open paren int close paren three point nine. That assigns three to x, not four. The cast truncates. It drops the decimal part, it does not round. This is the single most common gotcha in CS A. Second — implicit widening. double y equals five. Java automatically widens the int five into the double five point zero. No cast needed.

Printing. System dot out dot println of two plus three prints the number five. Java sees two numbers and an operator, so it computes. Now wrap two plus three in double quotes. System dot out dot println of quote two plus three quote prints the literal text two plus three. Quotes turn anything into a String. Without quotes, Java computes. With quotes, Java just prints.

Recap. Java is compiled — source to bytecode to JVM. Four primitives to memorize: int, double, boolean, char. Declare with type space name equals value semicolon. The cast int truncates, not rounds. println prints. Quotes mean String. Your assignment is in the dashboard — write a short Java program that declares your age, your GPA, and an isHonorRoll boolean, and prints all three.

Reminder — this video is the overview. To actually master primitive types, here's the rest. Read the Primitive Types chapter on CodeHS or Runestone — it has interactive code blocks. Practice on Codingbat or repl.it — write ten tiny programs, one for each new idea today. Submit the assignment in your dashboard. Book a check-in with your advisor if anything feels unclear. See you in Module Two — Using Objects.
